title: Oryza Deal Raises Questions About Halcyon's Balance Sheet Priorities
body:
When Halcyon Genomics disclosed its acquisition of gene-editing firm Oryza Therapeutics last weekend, the headline number — $1.8 billion — got most of the attention. But a closer look at the financing structure is prompting questions among investors about how the San Francisco company plans to fund its other pipeline commitments.

Halcyon carries roughly $600 million in outstanding debt from a 2023 convertible note offering. The Oryza purchase, largely cash-funded, will draw down a significant portion of the company's liquidity reserves. Halcyon had $2.1 billion in cash and short-term investments as of its most recent quarterly filing.

The biotech's stock has shed nearly seven percent since the deal was announced Monday, underperforming the broader XBI index.

Analysts at Linden Capital Markets cut their price target on Halcyon shares from $84 to $76, citing "integration risk and near-term cash burn uncertainty." They noted that Oryza's lead program has not yet entered human trials and that the timeline to revenue remains speculative.

Ferrara, in a call with analysts Thursday, pushed back on concerns. "We ran a thorough financial stress test," he said. "We remain fully funded through the end of 2028 under conservative assumptions."

Oryza's founder, Dr. Vasquez, is expected to remain as a key figure in the combined organization.
